supplementEntry.name: Methylene Blue
supplementEntry.evidenceTierRationale: Multiple RCTs in healthy humans (Rodriguez 2016 n=26), surgical patients (Deng 2021 n=248, Zhang 2025 n=217), psychiatric populations (Alda 2016 n=37, Telch 2014 n=42), and Alzheimer's Phase 2-3 trials (Wischik 2015 n=321, Gauthier 2018 n=800). Phase 3 AD trials did not meet primary endpoints as randomized; benefits seen in post-hoc monotherapy subgroups only. Healthy cognitive enhancement limited to one small RCT.
supplementEntry.dosageRange: 0.5-4 mg/kg/day oral (15-280 mg depending on indication)
supplementEntry.optimalDuration: Single dose for acute cognitive effects; 6-18 months for neurodegenerative indications
studyPopulations: Mild to moderate Alzheimer's disease patients
mechanismsOfAction: Mitochondrial electron carrier (alternative electron shuttle from NADH to cytochrome c)
Cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) activity enhancement
Nitric oxide synthase and guanylate cyclase inhibition (cGMP pathway)

commonSideEffects: Temporary blue staining of skin/mucous membranes
contraindications: Concurrent use with serotonergic drugs (serotonin syndrome risk)
drugInteractions: SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome - FDA black box warning)
Cholinesterase inhibitors (may reduce efficacy of LMTM per Phase 3 data)

effectSizes.memoryRetrieval: 7% increase in correct responses (P=0.01) (Rodriguez 2016 RCT, n=26)
effectSizes.fmriAttentionResponse: Significant increase in bilateral insular cortex (Z=2.9-3.4, P=.01-.008) (Rodriguez 2016)
effectSizes.fearExtinctionRetention: Significant enhancement post-successful exposure (d=0.58 for QoL in PTSD, Zoellner 2017)
effectSizes.bipolarDepression: Significant improvement on MADRS (P=0.02) and HAM-D (P=0.05) (Alda 2016, n=37)
effectSizes.adCognitionPhase2: ADAS-cog treatment effect -5.42 units (corrected p=0.047) at 138mg/day in moderate AD (Wischik 2015, n=321)
effectSizes.adBrainAtrophyMonotherapy: Atrophy rate declined to normal elderly levels after 9 months of monotherapy (Gauthier 2018)

keyCitations.title: Multimodal Randomized Functional MR Imaging of the Effects of Methylene Blue in the Human Brain
Methylene blue reduces incidence of early postoperative cognitive disorders in elderly patients undergoing major non-cardiac surgery: An open-label randomized controlled clinical trial
Effects of post-session administration of methylene blue on fear extinction and contextual memory in adults with claustrophobia
Tau aggregation inhibitor therapy: an exploratory phase 2 study in mild or moderate Alzheimer's disease
Potential of Low Dose Leuco-Methylthioninium Bis(Hydromethanesulphonate) (LMTM) Monotherapy for Treatment of Mild Alzheimer's Disease
From Mitochondrial Function to Neuroprotection-an Emerging Role for Methylene Blue

mechanisms.mechanism: Mitochondrial electron carrier (alternative electron shuttle from NADH to cytochrome c)

evidence.title: From Mitochondrial Function to Neuroprotection-an Emerging Role for Methylene Blue
evidence.findings: MB reroutes electrons in mitochondrial ETC from NADH to cytochrome c, increasing Complex IV activity. Promotes mitochondrial activity while reducing oxidative stress. Mitigates neuroinflammation. Efficacy shown in models of stroke, ischemia, AD, PD, and TBI.
evidence.methodology: Comprehensive review of preclinical and mechanistic literature

evidence.title: Mitochondrial respiration as a target for neuroprotection and cognitive enhancement
evidence.findings: Low-dose USP MB increases mitochondrial respiration, resulting in memory enhancement and neuroprotection. Proposes MB as neurotherapeutic strategy to improve brain energy metabolism.
evidence.methodology: Mechanistic and translational review

evidence.title: Tau aggregation inhibitor therapy: an exploratory phase 2 study in mild or moderate Alzheimer's disease
evidence.findings: At 138 mg/day dose, significant treatment benefit in moderate AD on ADAS-cog (treatment effect: -5.42 units, corrected p=0.047) and in mild AD on rCBF (treatment effect: 1.97%, corrected p<0.001). Benefit maintained over 50 weeks.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, dose-finding Phase 2 trial in mild/moderate AD

evidence.title: Methylene Blue Inhibits Formation of Tau Fibrils but not of Granular Tau Oligomers: A Plausible Key to Understanding Failure of a Clinical Trial for Alzheimer's Disease
evidence.findings: MB reduces tau fibrils but increases granular tau oligomers. Since granular tau oligomers (not fibrils) drive neuronal death, this provides a mechanistic explanation for mixed Phase III results.
evidence.methodology: In vitro tau aggregation assays with structural analysis

mechanisms.mechanism: Nitric oxide synthase and guanylate cyclase inhibition (cGMP pathway)

evidence.title: Methylene blue and Alzheimer's disease
evidence.findings: MB attenuates formation of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles, partially repairs mitochondrial function impairments. Influences cholinergic, serotonergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmitter systems relevant to AD pathogenesis.
evidence.methodology: Comprehensive review of MB pharmacology in AD context

evidence.title: Methylene blue treatment for residual symptoms of bipolar disorder: randomised crossover study
evidence.findings: Active dose (195 mg) significantly improved depression on MADRS (P=0.02) and HAM-D (P=0.05) and anxiety on HAM-A (P=0.02). Well tolerated. Effects consistent with MAO-A inhibition and monoaminergic modulation.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind randomized crossover trial in bipolar patients on lamotrigine

benefits.specificClaim: Improves memory retrieval and sustained attention in healthy adults
benefits.replicationStatus: Single well-designed RCT with fMRI biomarker confirmation

evidence.title: Multimodal Randomized Functional MR Imaging of the Effects of Methylene Blue in the Human Brain
evidence.findings: Low-dose MB increased fMRI response in bilateral insular cortex during sustained attention (Z=2.9-3.4, P=.01-.008) and prefrontal/parietal/occipital cortex during short-term memory tasks (Z=2.9-4.2, P=.03-.0003). MB associated with 7% increase in correct responses during memory retrieval (P=.01).
evidence.methodology: Randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled fMRI trial in healthy adults

evidence.title: Methylene blue modulates functional connectivity in the human brain
evidence.findings: MB reduced cerebral blood flow in task-related network during visuomotor task and produced stronger resting-state functional connectivity in regions linking perception and memory functions.
evidence.methodology: Randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled fMRI connectivity analysis

benefits.specificClaim: Reduces postoperative delirium and early cognitive dysfunction in elderly surgical patients
benefits.replicationStatus: Replicated across two RCTs in different surgical populations

evidence.title: Methylene blue reduces incidence of early postoperative cognitive disorders in elderly patients undergoing major non-cardiac surgery: An open-label randomized controlled clinical trial
evidence.findings: MB group showed significantly lower POD incidence (7.3% vs 24.2%, OR=0.24, 95%CI: 0.11-0.53, p<0.001) and lower early POCD at day 7 (16.1% vs 40.2%, OR=0.30, 95%CI: 0.16-0.57, p<0.001).
evidence.methodology: Prospective, randomized, open-label RCT in elderly patients undergoing major non-cardiac surgery

evidence.title: Effect of intraoperative methylene blue on postoperative delirium in elderly patients undergoing joint replacement: a randomized controlled trial
evidence.findings: POD incidence: MB group 8.3% vs control 20.4% (HR=0.39, 95%CI: 0.173-0.858, P=0.024). More postoperative fever in MB group (16.5% vs 7.4%).
evidence.methodology: Randomized controlled trial in elderly patients undergoing joint replacement surgery

benefits.specificClaim: Enhances fear extinction retention and memory consolidation when given post-exposure
benefits.replicationStatus: Replicated across two RCTs (claustrophobia and PTSD)

evidence.title: Effects of post-session administration of methylene blue on fear extinction and contextual memory in adults with claustrophobia
evidence.findings: MB post-training enhanced memory retention of fear extinction when administered after successful exposure, and enhanced contextual memory independently. Deleterious when given after unsuccessful exposure.
evidence.methodology: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in adults with claustrophobia

evidence.title: Enhancing Extinction Learning in Posttraumatic Stress Disorder With Brief Daily Imaginal Exposure and Methylene Blue: A Randomized Controlled Trial
evidence.findings: MB post-exposure enhanced treatment response (NNT=7.5) and quality of life (d=0.58). MB effects facilitated by better working memory.
evidence.methodology: Randomized controlled trial in chronic PTSD patients

benefits.specificClaim: Potential disease-modifying effects via tau aggregation inhibition and mitochondrial enhancement in AD
benefits.replicationStatus: Phase 2 positive; Phase 3 primary endpoints not met (monotherapy subgroup post-hoc positive)

evidence.title: Tau aggregation inhibitor therapy: an exploratory phase 2 study in mild or moderate Alzheimer's disease
evidence.findings: At 138 mg/day dose, significant treatment benefit in moderate AD on ADAS-cog (treatment effect: -5.42 units, corrected p=0.047) and in mild AD on rCBF (treatment effect: 1.97%, corrected p<0.001). Benefit maintained over 50 weeks.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, dose-finding Phase 2 trial

evidence.title: Potential of Low Dose Leuco-Methylthioninium Bis(Hydromethanesulphonate) (LMTM) Monotherapy for Treatment of Mild Alzheimer's Disease
evidence.sampleSize: n=800 (monotherapy subgroup n=79 at 100mg BID, n=76 at 4mg BID)
evidence.findings: Monotherapy showed significant benefits on ADAS-cog, ADCS-ADL, MRI atrophy, and glucose uptake (p<0.025). Brain atrophy rate in monotherapy patients declined to that of normal elderly controls after 9 months. LMTM 4 mg BID may be as effective as higher doses.
evidence.methodology: Phase III trial post-hoc monotherapy cohort analysis

evidence.title: Concentration-Dependent Activity of Hydromethylthionine on Cognitive Decline and Brain Atrophy in Mild to Moderate Alzheimer's Disease
evidence.findings: Steep concentration-response at low plasma levels. Significant differences in cognitive decline and brain atrophy with above-threshold levels. Effect sizes halved as add-on therapy. Predicted optimal dose: 16 mg/day monotherapy.
evidence.methodology: Pharmacokinetic/exposure-response analysis from two Phase III trials

benefits.specificClaim: Improves residual depressive and anxiety symptoms in bipolar disorder

evidence.title: Methylene blue treatment for residual symptoms of bipolar disorder: randomised crossover study
evidence.findings: Active dose (195 mg) significantly improved depression on MADRS (P=0.02) and HAM-D (P=0.05) and anxiety on HAM-A (P=0.02). Effects on cognitive symptoms were not significant. Well tolerated.
evidence.methodology: Double-blind randomized crossover trial in bipolar patients stabilized on lamotrigine

safety.claim: Well-tolerated at therapeutic doses with >100 years of clinical use as FDA-grandfathered drug

evidence.title: From Mitochondrial Function to Neuroprotection-an Emerging Role for Methylene Blue
evidence.findings: At low doses (0.5-4 mg/kg), MB enhances mitochondrial function and is well tolerated. At doses >5 mg/kg, paradoxical pro-oxidant effects occur. Non-linear dose-response is a critical safety consideration.
evidence.methodology: Review of safety and dose-response literature

safety.claim: FDA black box warning for serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic medications

evidence.title: Methylene blue and Alzheimer's disease
evidence.findings: MB inhibits MAO-A and modulates serotonergic neurotransmission. This mechanism underlies the FDA-recognized risk of serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs, SNRIs, or other serotonergic agents.
evidence.methodology: Pharmacological review of MB mechanisms including MAO inhibition

safety.claim: Risk of severe hemolytic anemia in G6PD-deficient individuals

evidence.title: Mitochondrial respiration as a target for neuroprotection and cognitive enhancement
evidence.findings: G6PD deficiency is an absolute contraindication due to risk of hemolytic anemia. G6PD screening recommended before MB use in populations with higher prevalence.
evidence.methodology: Translational review of safety considerations

safety.claim: Increased postoperative fever incidence at 2 mg/kg IV but no serious adverse events

evidence.title: Effect of intraoperative methylene blue on postoperative delirium in elderly patients undergoing joint replacement: a randomized controlled trial
evidence.findings: Postoperative fever incidence higher in MB group (16.5% vs 7.4%) but no serious adverse events. Overall safety profile acceptable for perioperative use.
evidence.methodology: Randomized controlled trial with systematic adverse event monitoring

dosage.dosageRange: 0.5-4 mg/kg single oral dose for cognitive enhancement
dosage.claim: Single oral dose effective for acute memory and attention enhancement in healthy adults

evidence.title: Multimodal Randomized Functional MR Imaging of the Effects of Methylene Blue in the Human Brain
evidence.findings: Single dose produced measurable fMRI changes and 7% improvement in memory retrieval accuracy within hours of administration.
evidence.methodology: Randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled fMRI trial

dosage.dosageRange: 8-16 mg/day oral for Alzheimer's disease (LMTM formulation)
dosage.claim: Low-dose LMTM monotherapy showed optimal exposure-response in AD

evidence.title: Concentration-Dependent Activity of Hydromethylthionine on Cognitive Decline and Brain Atrophy in Mild to Moderate Alzheimer's Disease
evidence.findings: Steep concentration-response at low plasma levels with plateau at higher doses. Predicted optimal monotherapy dose: 16 mg/day. Effect sizes reduced by half as add-on to cholinesterase inhibitors.
evidence.methodology: Pharmacokinetic/exposure-response analysis pooling two Phase III trials

evidence.title: Oral Tau Aggregation Inhibitor for Alzheimer's Disease: Design, Progress and Basis for Selection of the 16 mg/day Dose in a Phase 3, Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Hydromethylthionine Mesylate
evidence.journal: Journal of Prevention of Alzheimer's Disease
evidence.findings: Confirmatory Phase 3 trial (LUCIDITY) designed using 16 mg/day dose based on prior exposure-response data. 12-month double-blind placebo-controlled phase followed by 12-month open-label extension.
evidence.methodology: Phase 3 trial design and dosage rationale paper
